summary: Joel comments on Ray Ozzie's writing about software platform pricing dynamics, arguing that Ozzie's sophisticated reasoning about finding the 'right' price point is really just a rationalization for not making platforms cheap and ubiquitous. Joel suggests the real unspoken reason is simpler: lowering prices on your only product creates a revenue hit that investors won't tolerate and could sink the company. The post is a brief but pointed critique of how business realities get dressed up in strategic language.
key_points: Ray Ozzie argued that platform pricing must maintain perceived value and create margins before ubiquity is achieved
Joel interprets this as a rationalization for not following through on making platforms cheap and ubiquitous
The unspoken constraint is that lowering prices on your only product threatens revenue and investor confidence
Business survival realities often hide behind sophisticated-sounding platform strategy arguments
money_quotes: To me this sounds like a fancy way of saying, "I groove all that stuff about how platforms need to be cheap and ubiquitous, but I can't bring myself to do it."
What he doesn't mention: if you lower the price on the only product you're selling, you have a revenue hit, which will not make your investors happy, and you may run out of money and have to close.
But that must be what he's thinking.
key_insight: Sophisticated arguments about platform pricing strategy often disguise a simple business reality: companies can't afford to make their only product cheap, no matter how much platform theory says they should.
